notes.anchor: ashes from that destroyed city, Gomorrah
notes.note: Gomorrah is one of the cities God destroys with fire and brimstone in Genesis 19, its name ever after a byword for total divine ruin. Stumbling onto ash in a dark doorway, Ishmael reaches for the most extreme biblical image of destruction, then deflates it by reading the inn-sign as a trap.

notes.anchor: great Black Parliament sitting in Tophet
notes.note: Tophet was a place in the valley of Hinnom outside Jerusalem where children were burned in sacrifice (2 Kings 23:10; Jeremiah 7:31), so cursed by the prophets that its name became a synonym for hell. Having blundered through the wrong door in the dark, Ishmael reaches for that infernal image to convey his own panic and confusion.

Euroclydon is the violent northeast gale that seizes the ship carrying Paul and drives it to wreck near Malta in Acts 27:14. Naming a New Bedford night-wind after it lifts an ordinary cold evening into something biblical and dangerous.

notes.anchor: Poor Lazarus there, chattering his teeth against the curbstone
notes.note: Lazarus and Dives come from Jesus's parable in Luke 16:19-31: a sore-covered beggar lies at the gate of a rich man (Dives is Latin for rich man), and after death their fortunes reverse. Ishmael casts the freezing beggar as Lazarus and the comfortable as Dives to measure the moral gulf between the street and the warm inn.

notes.anchor: like another cursed Jonah (by which name indeed they called him)
notes.note: In the Book of Jonah a prophet's flight from God brings a deadly storm on his ship, and the sailors name him the cause of their peril, so that Jonah became the sailors' word for a person who carries bad luck aboard. The grim old bar-man, dealing out drink from inside a whale's jaw, is nicknamed Jonah for the ruin he ladles out.

notes.anchor: made a good offing towards the land of Nod
notes.note: The land of Nod is the country east of Eden to which Cain is exiled after killing Abel (Genesis 4:16); by a pun on nod, to doze, it became an English idiom for sleep. Ishmael plays on both senses, drifting toward sleep while quietly echoing exile and wandering.

notes.anchor: interminable Cretan labyrinth of a figure
notes.note: The Cretan labyrinth is the maze Daedalus built for King Minos to hold the Minotaur, its winding passages a byword for bewildering complexity. Ishmael borrows the image for Queequeg's tattoos, whose interlocking lines seem as impossible to trace as the mythic maze.

notes.anchor: not like Canaan; a land, also, of corn and wine
notes.note: Canaan is the land flowing with milk and honey that God promises Israel throughout Exodus and Deuteronomy. Melville plays on the formula: New Bedford is a land of oil rather than corn and wine, its wealth dragged up from the sea instead of poured out by Providence.

notes.anchor: recklessly burn their lengths in spermaceti candles
notes.note: Spermaceti is the fine waxy oil drawn from the great cavity in a sperm whale's head; refined, it made the brightest and most costly candles before gas and electric light. Burning whole candles of it is Melville's shorthand for wealth pulled straight from the whale-fishery.

notes.anchor: stave my soul, Jove himself cannot
notes.note: Jove is the Roman name for Jupiter, king of the gods and hurler of the thunderbolt, the supreme power of the classical pantheon. By daring Jove rather than the Christian God, Ishmael frames his defiance as heroic and classical, setting mortal nerve against the highest divine authority of the ancient world.

A brevet was a military commission granting an officer a higher rank in name only, without the pay or real authority. Ishmael jokes that dying in a stove boat would make him immortal by brevet: an empty, honorary immortality with nothing behind the title.

Man-ropes are the steadying side-ropes rigged beside a ship's ladder or gangway to give a climber something to grip in a rolling sea. Their presence on the chapel's pulpit-ladder carries the running conceit that the whole building is a ship and the pulpit its commanding height.

notes.anchor: one of the smallest strands in the mighty cable of the Scriptures
notes.note: The Book of Jonah runs only four chapters and is among the shortest books of the Hebrew Bible, yet it carries outsized weight in Jewish, Christian, and Muslim tradition. Father Mapple calls its chapters yarns, rope-strands and sailors' tales at once, to pull a crew of seamen straight into scripture.

notes.anchor: sin of this son of Amittai
notes.note: Jonah 1:1 names the prophet in the Hebrew manner, Jonah the son of Amittai. Father Mapple's use of the full patronymic rather than the plain name signals that he is preaching close to the biblical text, not loosely retelling a folk tale.

Tarshish is the distant port Jonah flees toward when God orders him instead to Nineveh (Jonah 1:3). Mapple's identification of it with Cadiz in Spain places Jonah's escape at the far western edge of the ancient world, as far from his duty as a ship can carry him.

Casting lots meant throwing marked tokens to read a verdict by chance, an ancient way of seeking God's will used throughout the Hebrew Bible. In Jonah 1:7 the storm-tossed sailors cast lots to find who has brought the disaster, and the lot falls on Jonah, which they take as a judgment straight from heaven.

notes.anchor: the ears of a wicked Nineveh
notes.note: Nineveh was the capital of Assyria, one of ancient Israel's most feared enemies, and God's command in Jonah 1:2 is to go there and cry out against its wickedness. Jonah is refusing not just a personal errand but a mission into the heart of the most hostile power of his world.

notes.anchor: while preaching to others is himself a castaway
notes.note: Father Mapple echoes 1 Corinthians 9:27, where Paul fears that after preaching to others he might himself become a castaway, rejected by God. Calling him the great Pilot Paul, Mapple folds the word into his sea-language so that a castaway is at once a shipwrecked sailor and a soul lost to grace.
